skill.name: test-coverage-analyzer
skill.description: Analyzes test coverage reports, identifies uncovered code paths, and suggests targeted tests for JavaScript, Python, Go, Ruby, and Java projects.
skill.summary: Finds coverage gaps and recommends focused tests for untested code paths.
skill.author: CrazyDubya

security_audit.summary: AI review found no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection. Most high static findings are false positives from Markdown examples, placeholder templates, or words such as key and errors. The real risk is legitimate local Bash and coverage-tool execution, which should be run only in trusted workspaces.

medium_findings.title: Local Coverage Commands Execute Project Tooling
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends commands such as npm, pytest, go test, and Maven coverage tasks. These commands are normal for coverage analysis, but they execute local project test code and build scripts.
medium_findings.recommendation: Run coverage commands only in trusted repositories or after user approval. Avoid running tests from untrusted code automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically tied to legitimate coverage workflows. Risk remains because test commands can execute arbitrary project code.

low_findings.title: Bash Helper Reads Local Coverage Files
low_findings.description: The helper script reads local coverage files and checks for common report paths. Arguments are quoted, and no network transfer or secret collection was found.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep file arguments quoted and restrict use to expected coverage report files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly performs local file checks and parsing. The implementation does not show exfiltration or unsafe unquoted command construction.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Patterns Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Scanner hits for weak cryptography, sensitive keys, path traversal, and reconnaissance map to Markdown examples or placeholder test templates. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, credential access, or malicious traversal.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep templates clearly marked as examples and avoid placeholder text that resembles dangerous patterns when practical.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these lines are documentation or test skeletons. They do not implement weak crypto, credential access, or traversal behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command findings are inline Markdown names for frameworks, report paths, or example commands. They are not Ruby backtick execution and do not run by themselves.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change is required for Markdown formatting. Review actual command execution separately.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown prose and code formatting, not executable Ruby. The only executable component is the separate Bash script.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Tool Invocation In Bash Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper invokes jq, lcov, grep, coverage, go, awk, sort, tail, and wc to parse local coverage reports. This is expected behavior, but it depends on local tools and trusted input files.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Treat the script as local developer tooling and run it only with expected coverage files from trusted projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms direct tool invocation. The commands are quoted and local, so the concern is operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams often know coverage is low but do not know which tests to write first. This skill reviews coverage reports and turns missing paths into prioritized test suggestions.
actual_capabilities: Detects common testing frameworks across JavaScript, Python, Ruby, Go, and Java projects.
Locates standard coverage reports such as lcov.info, coverage JSON, .coverage, and coverage.out.
Summarizes line, branch, function, and statement coverage when report data is available.
Identifies uncovered files, functions, branches, and error paths that need tests.
Suggests specific test cases and optional test stubs using included language templates.
limitations: It depends on existing coverage reports or user-approved coverage commands.
It cannot prove behavior correctness from coverage percentages alone.
Generated test stubs require project-specific imports, fixtures, and assertions.
Coverage command execution should be avoided in untrusted repositories.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Coverage Work Before Release
use_cases.description: Review coverage reports and rank missing tests by business risk and expected impact.

use_cases.title: Find Missing Unit Tests
use_cases.description: Map uncovered lines and branches to concrete unit test ideas for active feature work.

use_cases.title: Improve Pull Request Test Plans
use_cases.description: Turn coverage gaps into review comments and follow-up test tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Coverage Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the coverage report in this project and summarize the largest gaps in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Suggest Tests For Low Coverage Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Find files below 80 percent coverage and suggest the most useful tests for each file.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Prioritized Test Backlog
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the coverage data and source code to create a prioritized backlog of missing tests by risk and effort.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Framework-Specific Test Stubs
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate test stubs for the highest priority coverage gaps using the project test framework and existing test style.

output_examples.input: Analyze coverage for a service with low branch coverage.
output: Overall coverage is acceptable, but branch coverage is weak in payment validation.
Add tests for declined cards, expired cards, retry limits, and malformed payloads.
Start with payment validation because it handles customer-facing failure paths.

output_examples.input: Suggest tests for a Python module with uncovered exception paths.
output: Add tests for missing configuration, invalid input types, and downstream service errors.
Use fixtures for valid defaults, then override one field per failure case.
Confirm both the raised exception and the user-facing error message.

output_examples.input: Create a coverage improvement plan for a pull request.
output: The fastest improvement is testing public helpers with uncovered branches.
The highest risk gap is authentication token expiry handling.
Defer generated types and simple constants because they add little behavioral value.

best_practices: Review source code next to coverage data before accepting suggested tests.
Prioritize business logic, error handling, and security-sensitive paths first.
Run generated stubs through the project test suite and refine assertions manually.
anti_patterns: Do not chase 100 percent coverage without checking test value.
Do not run coverage commands automatically in untrusted repositories.
Do not accept placeholder imports or assertions without adapting them to the project.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests automatically?
faq.answer: It can suggest coverage commands, but users should approve execution because tests run local project code.

faq.question: Which coverage formats are supported?
faq.answer: It describes workflows for lcov.info, Jest coverage JSON, Python .coverage data, and Go coverage.out files.

faq.question: Can it generate complete test files?
faq.answer: It can draft stubs and examples, but project-specific imports, fixtures, and assertions still need review.

faq.question: Does high coverage mean the code is well tested?
faq.answer: No. The skill treats coverage as a signal and recommends tests that verify meaningful behavior.

faq.question: Can it work without a coverage report?
faq.answer: It can guide users to create one, but accurate gap analysis needs coverage data or source review.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning that local coverage scripts and test commands should run only in trusted workspaces.
